analysis_result: Model reproduced 75% (6/8) for 19p gain frequency in MPNST-G1 using Python CONUMEE-substitute, vs. paper-reported 62.5% (5/8) using the R/Bioconductor CONUMEE algorithm. The difference is exactly one sample out of eight (relative difference = 20%, exceeding the +/-10% concordance threshold).
discrepancy_analysis:
A. DATA LOADED: Methylation beta values for MPNST-G1 cohort (n=8) from GSE207207 IDAT files; CNV inference performed on chromosome 19p arm. The paper used CONUMEE (R/Bioconductor) per Methods (see Fig 1h caption: 'CNV heatmap generated from raw CONUMEE calls on methylation data'). The model used a Python CONUMEE-substitute (not the same R package).

B. COHORT DEFINITION: MPNST-G1 = n=8 high-grade MPNSTs forming the G1 methylation cluster (SHH pathway). Matches paper Fig 1 definition.

C. VARIABLE CODING: Outcome = copy number gain of chromosome arm 19p. In MPNST-G1. Numerator/denominator: paper = 5/8 (62.5%); model = 6/8 (75%).

D. COMPUTATION: Paper used CONUMEE v1.x (Bioconductor) with log2 ratios computed against a control reference set, segmentation, and arm-level calls with specific thresholds (typically log2 ratio > 0.1 for gain, < -0.1 for loss, though CONUMEE uses more nuanced thresholding). Python CONUMEE-substitute likely differs in: (1) reference panel construction, (2) probe-to-segment aggregation, (3) log2 ratio cutoff for gain calls, and/or (4) which probes on chromosome 19p are included (CONUMEE filters specific probes and uses a fixed bin structure). On a cohort of only 8 samples, a single sample flipping from 'no-gain' to 'gain' status due to slightly different thresholds shifts the rate from 62.5% to 75%.

E. RESULT: Reported 62.5% (5/8); Model 75% (6/8). Absolute difference 12.5 percentage points; relative difference 20%. One sample difference.

F. CAVEATS: (i) Python substitute is not algorithmically identical to R/Bioconductor CONUMEE; (ii) Paper does not specify exact log2 thresholds used, so any substitute method's threshold is inherently a judgment call; (iii) On n=8, every sample is 12.5% of the rate -- borderline arm-level calls are highly sensitive to method choice.

G. ROOT CAUSE: The discrepancy is driven by statistical/algorithmic method differences between the published CONUMEE (R/Bioconductor) pipeline and the Python substitute. Differences in reference construction, segmentation, and gain/loss thresholding can flip a single borderline 19p call, which on n=8 shifts the rate by exactly one sample (12.5 percentage points).
proposed_fix: Run the analysis using the actual R/Bioconductor CONUMEE package (see analyze-data recipe conumee_cnv.R at /home/klkehl/Partners HealthCare Dropbox/Kenneth Kehl/chatbpc/multi_paper_analyses/onc-data-wrangler-plugin/skills/analyze-data/recipes/R/conumee_cnv.R) rather than a Python substitute, to match the paper's exact arm-level calling (reference panel, segmentation, and gain/loss thresholds). Alternatively, calibrate the Python substitute's log2 ratio gain threshold against published CONUMEE calls for a held-out set of samples to match the paper's call boundary -- likely the Python method is using a slightly more permissive gain threshold that flips one 19p call from neutral to gain.
